title: HTTP Request-Response Cycle — Web Communication
examTip: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol, Port 80) and HTTPS (Port 443) follow a simple request-response model. Client sends a request with: Method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) + URL + Headers + Body. Server responds with: Status Code + Headers + Body. HTTP is STATELESS — each request is independent. Cookies/sessions are used to maintain state. HTTP/1.1 uses persistent connections; HTTP/2 uses multiplexing (multiple requests in one TCP connection); HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP-based).

blocks.label: HTTP Request-Response Cycle — Client-Server Web Communication
Body: (empty for GET) / JSON/FormData for POST
HTTP Methods & Common Status Codes
sublabels: Methods: GET (retrieve) | POST (create) | PUT/PATCH (update) | DELETE (remove) | HEAD (headers only) | OPTIONS (CORS preflight)
3xx Redirect: 301 Moved Permanently | 302 Found (temp) | 304 Not Modified (cached)
4xx Client Error: 400 Bad Request | 401 Unauthorized | 403 Forbidden | 404 Not Found | 429 Too Many Requests
5xx Server Error: 500 Internal Server Error | 502 Bad Gateway | 503 Service Unavailable | 504 Gateway Timeout

theory:
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is an application-layer protocol used for web communication. It follows a stateless client-server request-response model over TCP.

Key HTTP Versions:
- HTTP/1.0: New TCP connection per request (slow — one request at a time).
- HTTP/1.1: Persistent connections (keep-alive) — multiple requests on same TCP connection. Pipelining (partially). Head-of-line blocking problem.
- HTTP/2: Binary framing, request multiplexing (multiple requests in parallel on one TCP connection), header compression (HPACK), server push.
- HTTP/3 (QUIC): Built on UDP with built-in TLS 1.3, eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking, faster connection establishment.

Request Structure:
- Request Line: METHOD URL HTTP/version
- Headers: Key-value metadata (Host, Content-Type, Accept, Cookie, Authorization, etc.)
- Blank line (CRLF)
- Body: Optional — present in POST, PUT, PATCH requests.

Response Structure:
- Status Line: HTTP/version STATUS_CODE REASON
- Headers: Content-Type, Content-Length, Cache-Control, Set-Cookie, Location, etc.
- Blank line
- Body: HTML, JSON, binary data, etc.

Statelessness: HTTP does not remember previous requests. State is maintained externally via Cookies (client-side), Sessions (server-side), or JWT tokens.
keyPoints: HTTP (port 80) / HTTPS (port 443) — stateless request-response protocol over TCP.
Request: Method + URL + Headers + Body. Response: Status Code + Headers + Body.
GET = retrieve, POST = create, PUT/PATCH = update, DELETE = remove.
Status codes: 1xx Info, 2xx Success, 3xx Redirect, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error.
HTTP is STATELESS — cookies and sessions maintain state across requests.
HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple requests in one TCP connection. HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP).
useCases: Web browser fetching pages from web servers (core of the World Wide Web).
REST APIs — JSON data exchange using HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE).
Debugging web applications using browser DevTools Network tab.
